lastPrompt: I see both  source and email address fields being logged. are these indepentant? where does each come from? please examamione the source starting at index.html to answer.

content.text:
Yes, they're independent. Here's where each comes from:

**`source`** (index.html:2234-2266) — always present in every payload.
- If URL has a query string (e.g. `?abc`), that becomes the source and is saved to `localStorage['meditation-source']`. A changed query also fires a `changed` log with the previous source.
- Otherwise, uses the previously stored value.
- If nothing is stored, generates a random 10-char zbase32 string and stores it.
- Effectively an anonymous per-device/campaign identifier. Assigned to `appSource` at startup (line 2266) and passed to `buildPostPayload` on every log.

**`email`** (index.html:1114-1198) — optional, only present if the user entered one.
- Read via `loadEmailAddress()` from `localStorage['meditation-email']`.
- Populated when the user submits the email dialog (prompted on first run via `maybePromptForEmail()` at line 2285, or opened from the menu at line 2141).
- User can Skip/Cancel/Delete — no email, no field in payload.

The join point is `buildPostPayload` (index.html:1288-1296): `source` is always set from the argument; `email` is conditionally added only if `loadEmailAddress()` returns a non-empty value. Neither derives from the other.

3	This Apps Script receives session data from the Meditation Timer app via POST and appends each session as a row in a Google Sheet.
4	
5	## Columns
6	
7	| Column | Field |
8	|--------|-------|
9	| A | Timestamp (server-side, when the row was written) |
10	| B | Action (`"session"`, `"install"`, or `"changed"` - see Action Types below) |
11	| C | Source (identifies which device/install sent the data) |

15	| G | Meditation (configured duration in seconds) |

18	| J | Lead In Completed (seconds of lead in actually completed) |
19	| K | Meditation Paused (seconds spent paused during meditation) |
20	| L | Meditation Completed (seconds of meditation actually completed) |
21	| M | Lead Out Completed (seconds of lead out actually completed) |
22	| N | Meditation Logged (seconds the user chose to log, may include additional time) |
23	| O | Email Address (optional, provided by the user) |
24	
25	## Action Types
26	
27	| Action | When it fires | Fields populated |
28	|--------|---------------|------------------|
29	| `session` | User logs a meditation session (clicks "Yes" or the "+" additional time button) | Source (C), session fields (D-N), and optional Email Address (O) |
30	| `install` | User installs the PWA on their device | Source (C) and optional Email Address (O); columns D-N are blank |
31	| `changed` | A URL query string overwrites an existing source | Source (C) contains the old source, Email Address (O) is optional, and columns D-N are blank |
32	
33	If you already have an older sheet without the email column, the updated script rewrites row 1 with the new header set before appending data.

39	1. Create a new Google Sheet (or open an existing one).
40	2. Open **Extensions > Apps Script**.
41	3. Delete any existing code in `Code.gs` and paste the contents of `appscript.js`.
42	4. Click **Save**.
43	5. Click **Deploy > New deployment**.
44	6. Set type to **Web app**.
45	7. Set **Execute as** to **Me**.
46	8. Set **Who has access** to **Anyone**.
47	9. Click **Deploy** and authorize when prompted.
48	10. Copy the **Web app URL** - this is the endpoint the timer app will POST to.

51

53	
54	```bash
55	curl https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbwoHsydcHlE90CxgfC3DkdGSFxV3GpI63bDn5Fedti_ZYvfvNL-Tfpzr0IVzZDCts5ZYA/exec

62	Google Apps Script returns a 302 redirect with the JSON response encoded in the redirect URL. This means:
63	
64	- From the browser (`fetch`): works naturally - the browser handles the redirect and the script executes.
65	- From curl: do not use `-L` (follow redirects) because curl will replay the POST to a different host and fail. Instead, fire the POST and check the response headers.

# Meditation Timer - Google Sheets Logging

This Apps Script receives session data from the Meditation Timer app via POST and appends each session as a row in a Google Sheet.

## Columns

| Column | Field |
|--------|-------|
| A | Timestamp (server-side, when the row was written) |
| B | Action (`"session"`, `"install"`, or `"changed"` - see Action Types below) |
| C | Source (identifies which device/install sent the data) |
| D | Date (ISO string from the client when the session started) |
| E | Version (data format version) |
| F | Lead In (configured duration in seconds) |
| G | Meditation (configured duration in seconds) |
| H | Lead Out (configured duration in seconds) |
| I | Lead In Paused (seconds spent paused during lead in) |
| J | Lead In Completed (seconds of lead in actually completed) |
| K | Meditation Paused (seconds spent paused during meditation) |
| L | Meditation Completed (seconds of meditation actually completed) |
| M | Lead Out Completed (seconds of lead out actually completed) |
| N | Meditation Logged (seconds the user chose to log, may include additional time) |
| O | Email Address (optional, provided by the user) |

## Action Types

| Action | When it fires | Fields populated |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| `session` | User logs a meditation session (clicks "Yes" or the "+" additional time button) | Source (C), session fields (D-N), and optional Email Address (O) |
| `install` | User installs the PWA on their device | Source (C) and optional Email Address (O); columns D-N are blank |
| `changed` | A URL query string overwrites an existing source | Source (C) contains the old source, Email Address (O) is optional, and columns D-N are blank |

If you already have an older sheet without the email column, the updated script rewrites row 1 with the new header set before appending data.

The script now writes to the first sheet in the spreadsheet by default. If you want a specific tab, set `SHEET_NAME` in `appscript.js`.

## Setup

1. Create a new Google Sheet (or open an existing one).
2. Open **Extensions > Apps Script**.
3. Delete any existing code in `Code.gs` and paste the contents of `appscript.js`.
4. Click **Save**.
5. Click **Deploy > New deployment**.
6. Set type to **Web app**.
7. Set **Execute as** to **Me**.
8. Set **Who has access** to **Anyone**.
9. Click **Deploy** and authorize when prompted.
10. Copy the **Web app URL** - this is the endpoint the timer app will POST to.

## Testing

You can verify the endpoint is active with a GET request:

```bash
curl https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbwoHsydcHlE90CxgfC3DkdGSFxV3GpI63bDn5Fedti_ZYvfvNL-Tfpzr0IVzZDCts5ZYA/exec
```

Expected response: `{"status":"ok","message":"Meditation Timer endpoint is active."}`

### Testing POST with curl

Google Apps Script returns a 302 redirect with the JSON response encoded in the redirect URL. This means:

- From the browser (`fetch`): works naturally - the browser handles the redirect and the script executes.
- From curl: do not use `-L` (follow redirects) because curl will replay the POST to a different host and fail. Instead, fire the POST and check the response headers.

**bash/macOS/Linux:**

```bash
curl -s -D - -o /dev/null -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"action":"session","source":"test","email":"person@example.com","date":"2026-03-02T12:00:00Z","v":3,"leadIn":60,"meditation":600,"leadOut":60,"leadInPaused":0,"leadInCompleted":60,"meditationPaused":0,"meditationCompleted":600,"leadOutCompleted":60,"meditationLogged":600}' https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbwoHsydcHlE90CxgfC3DkdGSFxV3GpI63bDn5Fedti_ZYvfvNL-Tfpzr0IVzZDCts5ZYA/exec
```

**Windows cmd:**

```bash
curl -s -D - -o NUL -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d "{\"action\":\"session\",\"source\":\"test\",\"email\":\"person@example.com\",\"date\":\"2026-03-02T12:00:00Z\",\"v\":3,\"leadIn\":60,\"meditation\":600,\"leadOut\":60,\"leadInPaused\":0,\"leadInCompleted\":60,\"meditationPaused\":0,\"meditationCompleted\":600,\"leadOutCompleted\":60,\"meditationLogged\":600}" https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbwoHsydcHlE90CxgfC3DkdGSFxV3GpI63bDn5Fedti_ZYvfvNL-Tfpzr0IVzZDCts5ZYA/exec
```

A `302` response means the script executed successfully. To see the actual JSON response, GET the URL from the `Location` header. A new row should appear in the spreadsheet.

## Redeploying

After making changes to the script:

1. Open **Deploy > Manage deployments**.
2. Click the edit (pencil) icon on the active deployment.
3. Set **Version** to **New version**.
4. Click **Deploy**.

The URL stays the same. You must create a new version for changes to take effect.

# Timer App 

## Architecture

Single Page Web App. Designed to look simple and modern - especially on mobile. Uses cookies to store user state including an optional user-id and usage history.

Easily installable on homepage on mobile.  Uses js libs to keep phone from falling asleep after user presses start button. 

On first startup, the app asks for an optional email address. If provided, the app includes it with spreadsheet logging so usage can be studied. The user can later update or delete it from the menu.

## UI

all page transitions are smoothed with 250ms fade-in and fade-out. 

### Start page

Start page is simple and modern and sparse, large simple font. White letters, back background. 

There a three fillable fields (all in minutes 0-999) for ...
* Lead in (default 0)
* Mediation (default 20)
* lead out (default 0)

All fields are persistent via cookies. 

After loading for the first time, the app shows a dialog asking "Can I have your email address?" with an email field and skip/submit actions. Sharing is optional.

There is a START button.

If the service worker has already staged a newer version of the app, the start page also shows an update button. Pressing it reloads into the staged version.

When the user presses start, we fade out the start page and enter the timer page.

### Timer page

The mediation page has a large count-down timers with mins:secs in the center of the screen. Simple font, white text over the prescribed background image. The image is cropped and scaled so that it always fills the screen at its fixed aspect ratio. 

There is always a "pause" button below the count down text that will pause the current timer. While paused, the text of the remaining time subtly heartbeats between 100% and 50% brightness. 

There is always an "end" below the pause button. Pressing end button stops the timer and then smoothly brings us to the completion page with a fade-out and fade-in.

If the timer reaches 0:00 then we fade out and go to the completion page. 

# lead-in screen

If there was a non-zero lead-in time set, then we fade-in `sand.jpg` background image and then the lead in counts down. At the end of the lead-in (0:00), we play `knock.mp3`, fade out the image, and move on to the meditation screen. 

# meditation screen

we fade-in `sea.jpg` and count down the meditation timeout. When the timer expires (0:00), we play `gong.mp3` and fade out.

# lead-out screen

If a lead out time is non-zero, we fade in `sand.jpg` and count down the lead-out time. At the end (0:00), we play `knock.mp3` and fade out. 

### Completion page

This shows the most recently completed meditaion stats with text "Log this session?" and yes and no buttons. Pressing either button returns to the start page.

## Session Logging

When a session is logged, the app POSTs session data to a Google Apps Script endpoint that appends a row to a Google Sheet. The endpoint URL is hardcoded as `SHEET_URL`. Setting it to an empty string disables logging.

Three event types are logged:
- **session** — when the user logs a meditation session
- **install** — when the user installs the PWA (via the browser `appinstalled` event)
- **changed** — when a URL query string overwrites an existing source identifier (logs the old source)

If the user has provided an email address, it is included in the spreadsheet POST payload.

### Offline queue

POSTs are queued in localStorage (`meditation-post-queue`) and retried automatically:
- Immediately after queuing
- On page load
- When the browser comes back online (`online` event)

## App Updates

The service worker treats `index.html` as the update signal. Each time the start page becomes active, the page asks the service worker whether a staged update is already available. If not, the service worker starts a background fetch of `index.html`; when it detects a change, it stages the full asset set into a pending cache and marks the update as available for the next time the start page appears.

questions.question: For existing users who already have a 'meditation-source' stored, how should we initialize their new user-id?

options.label: Migrate source → user-id
options.description: Reuse the existing meditation-source value as their new user-id (preserves continuity so returning users keep the same identifier).

options.label: Generate fresh user-id
options.description: Ignore the old stored source; every existing user gets a new random user-id on next load (clean break, but loses continuity in the spreadsheet).

questions.question: What URL format should group-id use?

options.label: Bare query (?groupname)
options.description: Match current behavior — whole query string becomes the group-id. Simplest and backward-compatible for people sharing existing links.

options.label: Named param (?group-id=name)
options.description: Use an explicit parameter name. More extensible for future params but breaks any existing shared URLs.

questions.question: The current 'changed' log action fires when a URL overrides a stored source. With the split, what should happen?

options.label: Repurpose for group-id
options.description: Fire a 'changed' log (with old group-id as the source/group-id field) when a URL replaces the stored group-id. Mirrors current behavior for the new field.

options.label: Remove 'changed' action
options.description: Drop the 'changed' action entirely. Simpler, but loses visibility into group switches.

questions.question: Should I also update the server side (appscript.js, appscript.md, spec.md) to rename Source→User ID and add a Group ID column?

options.label: Yes, update everything
options.description: Rename Source header → User ID, add Group ID column in appscript.js, and update documentation. You'll need to redeploy the Apps Script for it to take effect.

options.label: Client only
options.description: Just modify index.html. You'll handle server-side changes and docs separately.

# Meditation Timer - Google Sheets Logging

This Apps Script receives session data from the Meditation Timer app via POST and appends each session as a row in a Google Sheet.

## Columns

| Column | Field |
|--------|-------|
| A | Timestamp (server-side, when the row was written) |
| B | Action (`"session"`, `"install"`, or `"changed"` - see Action Types below) |
| C | Source (identifies which device/install sent the data) |
| D | Date (ISO string from the client when the session started) |
| E | Version (data format version) |
| F | Lead In (configured duration in seconds) |
| G | Meditation (configured duration in seconds) |
| H | Lead Out (configured duration in seconds) |
| I | Lead In Paused (seconds spent paused during lead in) |
| J | Lead In Completed (seconds of lead in actually completed) |
| K | Meditation Paused (seconds spent paused during meditation) |
| L | Meditation Completed (seconds of meditation actually completed) |
| M | Lead Out Completed (seconds of lead out actually completed) |
| N | Meditation Logged (seconds the user chose to log, may include additional time) |
| O | Email Address (optional, provided by the user) |

## Action Types

| Action | When it fires | Fields populated |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| `session` | User logs a meditation session (clicks "Yes" or the "+" additional time button) | Source (C), session fields (D-N), and optional Email Address (O) |
| `install` | User installs the PWA on their device | Source (C) and optional Email Address (O); columns D-N are blank |
| `changed` | A URL query string overwrites an existing source | Source (C) contains the old source, Email Address (O) is optional, and columns D-N are blank |

If you already have an older sheet without the email column, the updated script rewrites row 1 with the new header set before appending data.

The script now writes to the first sheet in the spreadsheet by default. If you want a specific tab, set `SHEET_NAME` in `appscript.js`.

## Setup

1. Create a new Google Sheet (or open an existing one).
2. Open **Extensions > Apps Script**.
3. Delete any existing code in `Code.gs` and paste the contents of `appscript.js`.
4. Click **Save**.
5. Click **Deploy > New deployment**.
6. Set type to **Web app**.
7. Set **Execute as** to **Me**.
8. Set **Who has access** to **Anyone**.
9. Click **Deploy** and authorize when prompted.
10. Copy the **Web app URL** - this is the endpoint the timer app will POST to.

## Testing

You can verify the endpoint is active with a GET request:

```bash
curl https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbwoHsydcHlE90CxgfC3DkdGSFxV3GpI63bDn5Fedti_ZYvfvNL-Tfpzr0IVzZDCts5ZYA/exec
```

Expected response: `{"status":"ok","message":"Meditation Timer endpoint is active."}`

### Testing POST with curl

Google Apps Script returns a 302 redirect with the JSON response encoded in the redirect URL. This means:

- From the browser (`fetch`): works naturally - the browser handles the redirect and the script executes.
- From curl: do not use `-L` (follow redirects) because curl will replay the POST to a different host and fail. Instead, fire the POST and check the response headers.

**bash/macOS/Linux:**

```bash
curl -s -D - -o /dev/null -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"action":"session","source":"test","email":"person@example.com","date":"2026-03-02T12:00:00Z","v":3,"leadIn":60,"meditation":600,"leadOut":60,"leadInPaused":0,"leadInCompleted":60,"meditationPaused":0,"meditationCompleted":600,"leadOutCompleted":60,"meditationLogged":600}' https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbwoHsydcHlE90CxgfC3DkdGSFxV3GpI63bDn5Fedti_ZYvfvNL-Tfpzr0IVzZDCts5ZYA/exec
```

**Windows cmd:**

```bash
curl -s -D - -o NUL -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d "{\"action\":\"session\",\"source\":\"test\",\"email\":\"person@example.com\",\"date\":\"2026-03-02T12:00:00Z\",\"v\":3,\"leadIn\":60,\"meditation\":600,\"leadOut\":60,\"leadInPaused\":0,\"leadInCompleted\":60,\"meditationPaused\":0,\"meditationCompleted\":600,\"leadOutCompleted\":60,\"meditationLogged\":600}" https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbwoHsydcHlE90CxgfC3DkdGSFxV3GpI63bDn5Fedti_ZYvfvNL-Tfpzr0IVzZDCts5ZYA/exec
```

A `302` response means the script executed successfully. To see the actual JSON response, GET the URL from the `Location` header. A new row should appear in the spreadsheet.

## Redeploying

After making changes to the script:

1. Open **Deploy > Manage deployments**.
2. Click the edit (pencil) icon on the active deployment.
3. Set **Version** to **New version**.
4. Click **Deploy**.

The URL stays the same. You must create a new version for changes to take effect.

# Timer App 

## Architecture

Single Page Web App. Designed to look simple and modern - especially on mobile. Uses cookies to store user state including an optional user-id and usage history.

Easily installable on homepage on mobile.  Uses js libs to keep phone from falling asleep after user presses start button. 

On first startup, the app asks for an optional email address. If provided, the app includes it with spreadsheet logging so usage can be studied. The user can later update or delete it from the menu.

## UI

all page transitions are smoothed with 250ms fade-in and fade-out. 

### Start page

Start page is simple and modern and sparse, large simple font. White letters, back background. 

There a three fillable fields (all in minutes 0-999) for ...
* Lead in (default 0)
* Mediation (default 20)
* lead out (default 0)

All fields are persistent via cookies. 

After loading for the first time, the app shows a dialog asking "Can I have your email address?" with an email field and skip/submit actions. Sharing is optional.

There is a START button.

If the service worker has already staged a newer version of the app, the start page also shows an update button. Pressing it reloads into the staged version.

When the user presses start, we fade out the start page and enter the timer page.

### Timer page

The mediation page has a large count-down timers with mins:secs in the center of the screen. Simple font, white text over the prescribed background image. The image is cropped and scaled so that it always fills the screen at its fixed aspect ratio. 

There is always a "pause" button below the count down text that will pause the current timer. While paused, the text of the remaining time subtly heartbeats between 100% and 50% brightness. 

There is always an "end" below the pause button. Pressing end button stops the timer and then smoothly brings us to the completion page with a fade-out and fade-in.

If the timer reaches 0:00 then we fade out and go to the completion page. 

# lead-in screen

If there was a non-zero lead-in time set, then we fade-in `sand.jpg` background image and then the lead in counts down. At the end of the lead-in (0:00), we play `knock.mp3`, fade out the image, and move on to the meditation screen. 

# meditation screen

we fade-in `sea.jpg` and count down the meditation timeout. When the timer expires (0:00), we play `gong.mp3` and fade out.

# lead-out screen

If a lead out time is non-zero, we fade in `sand.jpg` and count down the lead-out time. At the end (0:00), we play `knock.mp3` and fade out. 

### Completion page

This shows the most recently completed meditaion stats with text "Log this session?" and yes and no buttons. Pressing either button returns to the start page.

## Session Logging

When a session is logged, the app POSTs session data to a Google Apps Script endpoint that appends a row to a Google Sheet. The endpoint URL is hardcoded as `SHEET_URL`. Setting it to an empty string disables logging.

Three event types are logged:
- **session** — when the user logs a meditation session
- **install** — when the user installs the PWA (via the browser `appinstalled` event)
- **changed** — when a URL query string overwrites an existing source identifier (logs the old source)

If the user has provided an email address, it is included in the spreadsheet POST payload.

### Offline queue

POSTs are queued in localStorage (`meditation-post-queue`) and retried automatically:
- Immediately after queuing
- On page load
- When the browser comes back online (`online` event)

## App Updates

The service worker treats `index.html` as the update signal. Each time the start page becomes active, the page asks the service worker whether a staged update is already available. If not, the service worker starts a background fetch of `index.html`; when it detects a change, it stages the full asset set into a pending cache and marks the update as available for the next time the start page appears.

**What changed:**

2. Pre-set `meditation-source=legacyval` → migrated to `meditation-user-id`, old key gone.
3. `?mygroup` → `meditation-group-id=mygroup`, no `changed` queued.

message.content: great. if we get a new group-id in the URL, what do we do?

content.text:
When a URL query is present, `initGroupId` (index.html:2262-2279) does this:

1. Read `prev` from `localStorage['meditation-group-id']`.
2. **If `prev` exists and is different from the new query** → queue a `changed` payload with `prev` as the `groupId` (carrying the OLD value), so the log can show what group they switched *from*.
3. Overwrite `localStorage['meditation-group-id']` with the new query.
4. Return the new value as `appGroupId`, so every payload from this load forward uses the new group-id.

Three scenarios fall out:

| Scenario | `changed` queued? | Storage |
|---|---|---|
| No prior group-id | No | New value saved |
| Same as stored | No (idempotent) | Unchanged |
| Different from stored | Yes, with **old** value | Overwritten with new |

The queued `changed` payload is flushed by `flushPostQueue()` (called at page init, on `online` events, and right after any queue push), so it lands in the sheet on the next successful POST.

content.text:
Two distinct things:

- **`meditationCompleted`** — measured truth. How many seconds of the meditation timer actually elapsed (ticked down). Capped at the configured meditation length. Set by the timer loop; ends at whatever value it reached when the user hit End or the timer ran out (index.html:1795).

- **`meditationLogged`** — the user's declared duration for the session. Defaults to `meditationCompleted` (line 1800). But if the user presses the **"+" additional-time button** on the completion screen, it becomes `meditationCompleted + addSec` (line 1959) — meaning "I actually meditated longer than the timer ran."

So if you set a 20-minute timer, hit End at 15 minutes, then pressed "+" to add 5 more minutes:
- `meditationCompleted` = 900 (15 min, what the timer counted)
- `meditationLogged` = 1200 (20 min, what you're claiming credit for)

In the normal case (no "+" pressed), the two are equal.

# Meditation Timer - Google Sheets Logging

This Apps Script receives session data from the Meditation Timer app via POST and appends each session as a row in a Google Sheet.

## Columns

| Column | Field |
|--------|-------|
| A | Timestamp (server-side, when the row was written) |
| B | Action (`"session"`, `"install"`, or `"changed"` - see Action Types below) |
| C | User ID (stable per-device identifier, randomly generated on first pageload) |
| D | Date (ISO string from the client when the session started) |
| E | Version (data format version) |
| F | Lead In (configured duration in seconds) |
| G | Meditation (configured duration in seconds) |
| H | Lead Out (configured duration in seconds) |
| I | Lead In Paused (seconds spent paused during lead in) |
| J | Lead In Completed (seconds of lead in actually completed) |
| K | Meditation Paused (seconds spent paused during meditation) |
| L | Meditation Completed (seconds of meditation actually completed) |
| M | Lead Out Completed (seconds of lead out actually completed) |
| N | Meditation Logged (seconds the user chose to log, may include additional time) |
| O | Email Address (optional, provided by the user) |
| P | Group ID (optional, supplied via URL query string and sticky across loads) |

## Action Types

| Action | When it fires | Fields populated |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| `session` | User logs a meditation session (clicks "Yes" or the "+" additional time button) | User ID (C), session fields (D-N), optional Email Address (O), optional Group ID (P) |
| `install` | User installs the PWA on their device | User ID (C), optional Email Address (O) and Group ID (P); columns D-N are blank |
| `changed` | A URL query string replaces the stored group-id | User ID (C) is the current user, Group ID (P) contains the **old** group-id, Email Address (O) is optional, and columns D-N are blank |

If you already have an older sheet without the email column, the updated script rewrites row 1 with the new header set before appending data.

The script now writes to the first sheet in the spreadsheet by default. If you want a specific tab, set `SHEET_NAME` in `appscript.js`.

## Setup

1. Create a new Google Sheet (or open an existing one).
2. Open **Extensions > Apps Script**.
3. Delete any existing code in `Code.gs` and paste the contents of `appscript.js`.
4. Click **Save**.
5. Click **Deploy > New deployment**.
6. Set type to **Web app**.
7. Set **Execute as** to **Me**.
8. Set **Who has access** to **Anyone**.
9. Click **Deploy** and authorize when prompted.
10. Copy the **Web app URL** - this is the endpoint the timer app will POST to.

## Testing

You can verify the endpoint is active with a GET request:

```bash
curl https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbztPI-tkV2t_d3e7QQv_WZ7kOL8QWu5cmrsss8vTc2W8bpJDX9MS4lXWPFV_0F5_LX3sw/exec
```

Expected response: `{"status":"ok","message":"Meditation Timer endpoint is active."}`

### Testing POST with curl

Google Apps Script returns a 302 redirect with the JSON response encoded in the redirect URL. This means:

- From the browser (`fetch`): works naturally - the browser handles the redirect and the script executes.
- From curl: do not use `-L` (follow redirects) because curl will replay the POST to a different host and fail. Instead, fire the POST and check the response headers.

**bash/macOS/Linux:**

```bash
curl -s -D - -o /dev/null -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"action":"session","userId":"test","groupId":"mygroup","email":"person@example.com","date":"2026-03-02T12:00:00Z","v":3,"leadIn":60,"meditation":600,"leadOut":60,"leadInPaused":0,"leadInCompleted":60,"meditationPaused":0,"meditationCompleted":600,"leadOutCompleted":60,"meditationLogged":600}' https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbztPI-tkV2t_d3e7QQv_WZ7kOL8QWu5cmrsss8vTc2W8bpJDX9MS4lXWPFV_0F5_LX3sw/exec
```

**Windows cmd:**


# Meditation Timer - Google Sheets Logging

This Apps Script receives session data from the Meditation Timer app via POST and appends each session as a row in a Google Sheet.

## Columns

| Column | Field |
|--------|-------|
| A | Timestamp (server-side, when the row was written) |
| B | Action (`"session"`, `"install"`, or `"changed"` - see Action Types below) |
| C | User ID (stable per-device identifier, randomly generated on first pageload) |
| D | Date (ISO string from the client when the session started) |
| E | Version (data format version) |
| F | Lead In (configured duration in seconds) |
| G | Meditation (configured duration in seconds) |
| H | Lead Out (configured duration in seconds) |
| I | Lead In Paused (seconds spent paused during lead in) |
| J | Lead In Completed (seconds of lead in actually completed) |
| K | Meditation Paused (seconds spent paused during meditation) |
| L | Meditation Completed (seconds of meditation actually completed) |
| M | Lead Out Completed (seconds of lead out actually completed) |
| N | Meditation Logged (seconds the user chose to log, may include additional time) |
| O | Email Address (optional, provided by the user) |
| P | Group ID (optional, supplied via URL query string and sticky across loads) |

## Action Types

Every row populates the common fields: User ID (C), Date (D), Version (E), and — when set — Email Address (O) and Group ID (P). Action-specific fields are described below.

| Action | When it fires | Action-specific fields |
|--------|---------------|------------------------|
| `session` | User logs a meditation session (clicks "Yes" or the "+" additional time button) | Session fields F-N (durations and completed/paused times); Date (D) reflects the session start time |
| `install` | User installs the PWA on their device | None; columns F-N are blank |
| `changed` | A URL query string replaces the stored group-id | Group ID (P) contains the **old** (pre-switch) group-id; columns F-N are blank |

If you already have an older sheet without the email column, the updated script rewrites row 1 with the new header set before appending data.

The script now writes to the first sheet in the spreadsheet by default. If you want a specific tab, set `SHEET_NAME` in `appscript.js`.

## Setup

1. Create a new Google Sheet (or open an existing one).
2. Open **Extensions > Apps Script**.
3. Delete any existing code in `Code.gs` and paste the contents of `appscript.js`.
4. Click **Save**.
5. Click **Deploy > New deployment**.
6. Set type to **Web app**.
7. Set **Execute as** to **Me**.
8. Set **Who has access** to **Anyone**.
9. Click **Deploy** and authorize when prompted.
10. Copy the **Web app URL** - this is the endpoint the timer app will POST to.

## Testing

You can verify the endpoint is active with a GET request:

```bash
curl https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbztPI-tkV2t_d3e7QQv_WZ7kOL8QWu5cmrsss8vTc2W8bpJDX9MS4lXWPFV_0F5_LX3sw/exec
```

Expected response: `{"status":"ok","message":"Meditation Timer endpoint is active."}`

### Testing POST with curl

Google Apps Script returns a 302 redirect with the JSON response encoded in the redirect URL. This means:

- From the browser (`fetch`): works naturally - the browser handles the redirect and the script executes.
- From curl: do not use `-L` (follow redirects) because curl will replay the POST to a different host and fail. Instead, fire the POST and check the response headers.

**bash/macOS/Linux:**

```bash
curl -s -D - -o /dev/null -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"action":"session","userId":"test","groupId":"mygroup","email":"person@example.com","date":"2026-03-02T12:00:00Z","v":3,"leadIn":60,"meditation":600,"leadOut":60,"leadInPaused":0,"leadInCompleted":60,"meditationPaused":0,"meditationCompleted":600,"leadOutCompleted":60,"meditationLogged":600}' https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbztPI-tkV2t_d3e7QQv_WZ7kOL8QWu5cmrsss8vTc2W8bpJDX9MS4lXWPFV_0F5_LX3sw/exec
```

**Windows cmd:**

```bash
curl -s -D - -o NUL -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d "{\"action\":\"session\",\"userId\":\"test\",\"groupId\":\"mygroup\",\"email\":\"person@example.com\",\"date\":\"2026-03-02T12:00:00Z\",\"v\":3,\"leadIn\":60,\"meditation\":600,\"leadOut\":60,\"leadInPaused\":0,\"leadInCompleted\":60,\"meditationPaused\":0,\"meditationCompleted\":600,\"leadOutCompleted\":60,\"meditationLogged\":600}" https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbztPI-tkV2t_d3e7QQv_WZ7kOL8QWu5cmrsss8vTc2W8bpJDX9MS4lXWPFV_0F5_LX3sw/exec
```

A `302` response means the script executed successfully. To see the actual JSON response, GET the URL from the `Location` header. A new row should appear in the spreadsheet.

## Redeploying

After making changes to the script:

1. Open **Deploy > Manage deployments**.
2. Click the edit (pencil) icon on the active deployment.
3. Set **Version** to **New version**.
4. Click **Deploy**.

The URL stays the same. You must create a new version for changes to take effect.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Timer App 

## Architecture

Single Page Web App. Designed to look simple and modern - especially on mobile. Uses cookies to store user state including an optional user-id and usage history.

Easily installable on homepage on mobile.  Uses js libs to keep phone from falling asleep after user presses start button. 

On first startup, the app asks for an optional email address. If provided, the app includes it with spreadsheet logging so usage can be studied. The user can later update or delete it from the menu.

## UI

all page transitions are smoothed with 250ms fade-in and fade-out. 

### Start page

Start page is simple and modern and sparse, large simple font. White letters, back background. 

There a three fillable fields (all in minutes 0-999) for ...
* Lead in (default 0)
* Mediation (default 20)
* lead out (default 0)

All fields are persistent via cookies. 

After loading for the first time, the app shows a dialog asking "Can I have your email address?" with an email field and skip/submit actions. Sharing is optional.

There is a START button.

If the service worker has already staged a newer version of the app, the start page also shows an update button. Pressing it reloads into the staged version.

When the user presses start, we fade out the start page and enter the timer page.

### Timer page

The mediation page has a large count-down timers with mins:secs in the center of the screen. Simple font, white text over the prescribed background image. The image is cropped and scaled so that it always fills the screen at its fixed aspect ratio. 

There is always a "pause" button below the count down text that will pause the current timer. While paused, the text of the remaining time subtly heartbeats between 100% and 50% brightness. 

There is always an "end" below the pause button. Pressing end button stops the timer and then smoothly brings us to the completion page with a fade-out and fade-in.

If the timer reaches 0:00 then we fade out and go to the completion page. 

# lead-in screen

If there was a non-zero lead-in time set, then we fade-in `sand.jpg` background image and then the lead in counts down. At the end of the lead-in (0:00), we play `knock.mp3`, fade out the image, and move on to the meditation screen. 

# meditation screen

we fade-in `sea.jpg` and count down the meditation timeout. When the timer expires (0:00), we play `gong.mp3` and fade out.

# lead-out screen

If a lead out time is non-zero, we fade in `sand.jpg` and count down the lead-out time. At the end (0:00), we play `knock.mp3` and fade out. 

### Completion page

This shows the most recently completed meditaion stats with text "Log this session?" and yes and no buttons. Pressing either button returns to the start page.

## Session Logging

When a session is logged, the app POSTs session data to a Google Apps Script endpoint that appends a row to a Google Sheet. The endpoint URL is hardcoded as `SHEET_URL`. Setting it to an empty string disables logging.

Three event types are logged:
- **session** — when the user logs a meditation session
- **install** — when the user installs the PWA (via the browser `appinstalled` event)
- **changed** — when a URL query string replaces the stored group-id (logs the old group-id)

If the user has provided an email address, it is included in the spreadsheet POST payload.

### Offline queue

POSTs are queued in localStorage (`meditation-post-queue`) and retried automatically:
- Immediately after queuing
- On page load
- When the browser comes back online (`online` event)

## App Updates

The service worker treats `index.html` as the update signal. Each time the start page becomes active, the page asks the service worker whether a staged update is already available. If not, the service worker starts a background fetch of `index.html`; when it detects a change, it stages the full asset set into a pending cache and marks the update as available for the next time the start page appears.

+Every logged event carries a common set of fields: `action`, `date`, `v` (log payload version), `userId`, plus `groupId` and `email` when set. Session events add the per-session duration/completion fields on top.

content.text:
Based on the documentation, here's what's likely happening with your diff view:

## Key Finding: Auto Mode + Auto-Accept Changes How Changes Are Displayed

**The diff view compares against `git HEAD`** (not a session baseline). In auto mode, when you approve edits with auto-accept, Claude executes them without showing a per-action approval prompt—but this doesn't prevent them from appearing in the diff view. However:

1. **The diff view is primarily designed for interactive review**. In auto mode, you're bypassing the step-by-step approval UI, which is where the diff review typically happens. The desktop app's diff panel might not be actively tracking pending edits when auto-accept is enabled.

2. **Edits are applied to disk immediately** (which you've verified), but the diff panel may only refresh when you explicitly trigger it or when the session transitions between phases.

## Troubleshooting Steps

1. **Manually refresh the diff view** - Look for a refresh button in the diff panel, or switch to a different tab and back.
2. **Run `git diff` in the terminal** - Verify the changes are actually staged/unstaged in git. The diff panel might be showing git's view, not pending-only.
3. **Check if auto mode is dropping the diff display** - Since auto mode skips permission prompts, the UI might not render diffs. Try switching to `acceptEdits` mode (press Shift+Tab) to see if diffs appear—this keeps auto-approval but preserves the interactive diff UI.
4. **Restart the session** - Close and reopen the Code tab; the diff view should refresh from disk.

The edits *are* applied correctly (you've confirmed via Read/Grep), so this is a UI display issue, not a data corruption problem.

content.text:
Git sees the changes fine, so this is purely a UI bug in the desktop app's diff panel.

**What's actually on disk** (confirmed by `git status`):
```
modified:   appscript.md
modified:   index.html
modified:   spec.md
```
With 40 insertions / 29 deletions across those 3 files — that's the logging refactor (createLogPayload/logSession/logInstall/logGroupChanged) plus the matching doc updates.

**Why `appscript.js` isn't in the list**: the earlier user-id/group-id header rename to appscript.js landed in commit `00b1d6b` ("Add `group-id` field") — which is already committed. So appscript.js is clean relative to HEAD.

**Why the panel says "No changes to show"**: the panel is titled `main → working tree` (i.e., HEAD vs. working tree), and git does report diffs there, so the panel is just stale. Try:

1. Close and reopen the diff panel (click the X in its header, then re-open).
2. Or restart the Claude Code desktop app.
3. Worst case: `git diff` in the terminal gives you the ground truth.

One other thing visible in the output: git warned `LF will be replaced by CRLF in appscript.md`. That's Windows line-ending normalization — not the cause of the UI issue, but worth knowing if you see diffs that look bigger than expected when you commit.
